event: Middle Stone Age hunter-gatherers inhabit the savannas and forested zones of present-day Togo, leaving behind stone tools.
Neolithic communities adopt settled agriculture, cultivating yams and millet across the region's plateaus and plains.
Iron-working spreads through the area, enabling more productive farming and the growth of larger village settlements.
Various ethnic groups, including ancestors of the Kabye and Tem, settle the northern hills and mountains as farming communities.
The Ewe people migrate westward from the Tado and Notsie regions, settling much of southern Togo.
Mina and Guin peoples migrate from the Gold Coast (modern Ghana) to settle the coastal area around Aneho and Glidji.
According to Ewe tradition, communities flee the walled city of Notsie to escape the tyrannical king Agokoli, dispersing across the south.
European and Atlantic slave traders intensify activity along the coast, which becomes part of the notorious 'Slave Coast.'
Coastal towns such as Aneho become centers of the Atlantic slave trade, where captives are bought and shipped to the Americas.
The expanding Kingdom of Dahomey raids and dominates parts of the coastal region in pursuit of captives and trade control.
Danish, Portuguese, and other European traders maintain forts and trading posts along the coast for the slave and goods trade.
Christian missionaries from the North German (Bremen) Missionary Society begin evangelizing and establishing schools among the Ewe.
German explorer Gustav Nachtigal signs a protectorate treaty with chief Mlapa III at Togoville, establishing the German colony of Togoland.
The Berlin Conference and the Scramble for Africa recognize German claims, formalizing Togoland's place in the European partition of Africa.
Germany establishes Lome as the colonial capital, developing it as a key administrative and commercial center.
Germany completes a railway and a wharf at Lome, promoting Togoland as a profitable 'model colony' built on forced labor and cash crops.
At the outbreak of World War I, British and French forces invade and German forces surrender Togoland after the Battle of Chra.
Britain and France divide Togoland between them, with France administering the larger eastern portion.
The League of Nations formally grants mandates over Togoland to France and Britain, dividing the former German colony.
Togoland becomes a United Nations Trust Territory, with France and Britain continuing as administering powers.
British Togoland votes in a UN-supervised plebiscite to join the Gold Coast, integrating into what becomes Ghana the following year.
Sylvanus Olympio's Comite de l'Unite Togolaise wins UN-supervised elections, paving the way toward independence for French Togoland.
Togo gains full independence from France on 27 April, with Sylvanus Olympio becoming its first president, and joins the United Nations.
President Olympio is assassinated in a military coup, becoming one of the first post-independence African leaders to be overthrown by force.
Lieutenant Colonel Gnassingbe Eyadema seizes power in a coup, banning political parties and beginning a decades-long authoritarian rule.
Eyadema founds the Rally of the Togolese People (RPT) as the sole legal political party, cementing one-party rule.
Eyadema launches an 'authenticity' campaign, nationalizing the phosphate industry and adopting an African name for himself.
A new constitution is adopted and Eyadema is confirmed president in single-candidate elections, formalizing one-party governance.
An attempted coup against Eyadema is put down with French and Zairean military assistance.
Pro-democracy protests erupt across Togo, forcing Eyadema to accept the principle of multiparty politics.
A National Conference strips Eyadema of much of his power and names a transitional government under interim prime minister Joseph Kokou Koffigoh.
Voters approve a new multiparty constitution, though Eyadema retains his grip on power amid violence and military pressure.
Eyadema wins a disputed presidential election boycotted by the opposition amid political violence that drives thousands into exile.
Eyadema is re-elected in an election marred by fraud allegations and reports of post-election killings.
The constitution is amended to remove presidential term limits, allowing Eyadema to extend his long tenure.
Eyadema dies after 38 years in power, and the military installs his son Faure Gnassingbe, triggering international condemnation and deadly unrest.
The government and opposition sign a Global Political Agreement in Ouagadougou aimed at ending the political crisis and reforming institutions.
Relatively peaceful legislative elections allow the European Union to resume aid suspended since 1993.
Faure Gnassingbe wins re-election as president, and the main opposition again disputes the results.
Mass anti-government protests demand presidential term limits and an end to the Gnassingbe family's decades-long rule.
Parliament reinstates a two-term presidential limit, but a non-retroactive provision allows Faure Gnassingbe to run for further terms.
Faure Gnassingbe wins a fourth term as president in elections the opposition denounces as fraudulent.
A new constitution shifts Togo to a parliamentary system, drawing accusations that it is a 'constitutional coup' to keep Gnassingbe in power.
Faure Gnassingbe is sworn in as President of the Council of Ministers, the powerful new top post created under the 2024 constitution.
